content: Civil – Civil Procedure Rules – Justice UK Cookie Settings Are you OK with cookies? We use small files called ‘cookies’ on www.justice.gov.uk. Some are essential to make the site work, some help us to understand how we can improve your experience, and some are set by third parties. You can choose to turn off the non-essential cookies. Which cookies are you happy for us to use? I am OK with cookies Only use essential cookies Choose which cookies we use Close Choose which cookies we use Marketing cookies Off On Google Analytics Off On We use Google Analytics to measure how you use the website so we can improve it based on user needs. We do not allow Google Analytics to use or share the data about how you use this site. See our Google Analytics cookies Google Analytics Name Purpose Expires _ga These help us count how many people visit justice.gov.uk by tracking if you’ve visited before 2 years _gid These help us count how many people visit justice.gov.uk by tracking if you’ve visited before 24 hours _gat These help us to manage how we collect analytics when we have lots of visitors on the site at one time 10 minutes Google Analytics Events Off On We use Google Analytics events to measure clicks within the pages and extended usage of the site See our Google Analytics Events cookies Google Analytics Events Name Purpose Expires _ga These help us count how many people visit justice.gov.uk by tracking if you’ve visited before 2 years Essential cookies Always on All users These cookies will always need to be on because they make our site work. See our All users cookies All users Name Purpose Expires wordpress_test_cookie This is used to test whether the browser accepts cookies When you close your browser PHPSESSID This is used to link your device to the information sent to the server from your browser. It is typically used to avoid you having to retype information when moving from one page to another. When you close your browser ccfw-banner-hidden Lets us know you have chosen which cookies are used so we can stop the cookie banner appearing when you return to the site. 1 year ccfw-gtm-allowed Lets us know you have accepted certain cookies so we can stop them loading when you return to the site. 1 year ccfw-time This is used to store the cookie expiry dates so we know when to ask you again for consent. 1 year ccfw_gtm_id This cookie is used to link the cookie consent banner to Google Tag Manager 1 year Logged in users These cookies apply when users log in. They will always be on because they make our site work. See our Logged in users cookies Logged in users Name Purpose Expires wordpress_[hash] This authenticates you when you log in to the admin area When you close your browser wordpress_logged_in This shows the site that you’re logged in and who you are so you can access the functions you need When you close your browser wordpress_sec If you are logged in as a site admin, this stores your authentication details. When you close your browser wp-settings-{time}-[UID] The number on the end [UID] is your individual user ID from the users database. 1 year > Save cookie preferences . Search Enter your civil procedure rules search. Civil Procedure (Amendment No. 3) Rules 2025 and the 192 nd  PD Update The Statutory Instrument (SI) is published via the legislation website (together with the explanatory memorandum which provides fuller detail of the changes). The rules online will be updated in due course. The Civil Procedure (Amendment No.3) Rules 2025 will enter into force, on 31 st December 2025 and contains amendments to give effect to the new power of the Secretary of State to direct the Parole Board to refer certain of its release decisions to the High Court under section 32ZAA of the Crime (Sentences) Act 1997 or section 256AZBA of the Criminal Justice Act 2003. CPR Part 77 (provisions in support of criminal justice) is amended. The 192nd Practice Direction (PD) Update  comes into effect on 31 st  December 2025.  The Master of the Rolls and the Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State have signed the Practice Direction Update, which contains a suite of amendments to PD77 (in support of the Civil Procedure (Amendment No 3) Rules 2025 (above). Concerning the procedure for referral of Parole Board release decisions to the High Court. PD Making Document (PDF) Proposed amendments as to provision concerning the Welsh language consultation The Civil Procedure Rules (CPR) and its attendant Practice Directions (PDs) contain a variety of provisions specifically directed to matters concerning Wales and the Welsh language. They are applicable in both Wales and England.  These provisions have been introduced and amended piecemeal over the years.  A review has been carried out by the Welsh judicial member of the CPRC, and that review of the rules and possible amendments have been considered by the CPRC. Read more on this, including how to submit your comments on GOV.UK. Fixed Recoverable Costs (FRC) Interim Implementation Stocktake The Civil Procedure Rule Committee is undertaking a consultation regarding the effectiveness of the extension of FRC, inviting feedback as part of an evidence-gathering initiative conducted in collaboration with the Ministry of Justice (MoJ).   Read more on this, including how to submit your comments on GOV.UK. The 191 st Practice Direction Update   The Master of the Rolls and the Minister of State for Justice have signed the 191 st  Practice Direction which comes into force on 1 st January 2026.   The 191 st  PD Update advances the principle of open justice in the civil courts by introducing a new pilot scheme (the Access to Public Domain Documents Pilot). The pilot is designed to test, in selected courts, a simpler process for accessing most documents which enter the public domain under the law of England and Wales, including skeleton arguments and witness statements. This will operate via the existing CE-File system and will be tested in the Commercial Court, London Circuit Commercial Court of the King’s Bench Division and the Financial List (Commercial Court and Chancery Division) only. It will operate for a two-year period. The pilot only applies to documents which enter the public domain via a hearing which takes place in public.   A guidance note for users has also been produced by the working group and has been issued to the pilot courts. PD Making Document (PDF) Traffic Enforcement consultation: proposed amendments to CPR Part 75 and Practice Direction 75 The CPRC is seeking views about proposed amendments to Part 75 and PD 75 which govern the procedures for traffic enforcement cases at the Traffic Enforcement Centre (TEC).  The aim of the proposed amendments is to make the process clearer, more efficient and fairer for both authorities and individuals. Read more on this, including how to submit your comments  on GOV.UK. The 190 th Practice Direction Update   The Master of the Rolls and the Minister of State for Justice have signed the 190 th Practice Direction which comes into force at 11am on 16 th September 2025.   The 190 th PD update amends PD51R (Online Civil Money Claims Pilot) which will expand “case progression” and “general applications” features to all County Courts. “Case Progression” is a term used to describe the stages that follow the making of an allocation order by either a legal adviser or judge. The 190 th PD update also makes minor amendments to both PD51R (OCMC) and PD51ZB (DCP) including modest tidying up namely: Correcting a typo relating to the end of pilot date in PD51R (restoring it to 1st October 2026). Enabling a legally represented claimant to discontinue part of their claim using the online system for both PD51R and PD51ZB; and Aligning provision in PD51R on the sending out of claims which are not suitable for OCMC with the equivalent provision in PD51ZB. 190th PD Making Document (PDF) Pre-Action Protocol (PAP) Update: PAP for Personal Injury Claims below the Small Claims Limit in Road Traffic Accidents (“The RTA Small Claims Protocol”) The Master of the Rolls, as Head of Civil Justice, has approved the amendments within this PAP update, which come into effect on 1 st October 2025. The amendments to the RTA Small Claims Protocol address how non-Protocol vehicle costs (NVC) are handled in the Official Injury Claim Portal. Instead of requiring claimants and compensators to upload NVC forms themselves the Portal will now create these forms automatically using information from both parties. PAP Update Document (PDF) The 189th  Practice Direction Update The Master of the Rolls and the Minister of State for Justice have signed the 189 th Practice Direction  which comes into force at 11am on 12 th August 2025.    The 189 th PD update amends PD 51R (Online Civil Money Claims Pilot) and PD 51ZB (Damages Claims Pilot). The amendments to PD 51R primarily ensure that a case can remain online after a Notice of Change is filed by a Legal Representative on behalf of a defendant in the Online Civil Money Claims Pilot.  Minor amendments are also being made to clarify (a) provision relating to the issuing of claims and limitation, (b) the headings to paragraphs 7.3 and 7.38 to improve readability, and (c) paragraph 6C.1(c) (as whether a claim has been submitted in Welsh is not relevant to the question as to whether it can remain within the pilot after directions have been made). In relation to the amendment to PD 51ZB, this omits paragraph 8.6 (relating to discontinuance).  This amendment was intended to be made as part of the 184 th update but was overlooked at the time.  It has accordingly been included in this update. 189th PD Making Document (PDF) The 188th  Practice Direction Update  The 188 th Practice Direction (PD) Update comes into effect on 1 st October 2025. The Master of the Rolls and the Courts Minister have signed the Practice Direction Update, which contains a suite of amendments (in addition to the those contained in the 186 th PD Update (below)) in support of the Civil Procedure (Amendment No 2) Rules 2025 (below), as follows:    Judicial review of nationally significant infrastructure projects (NSIPs) – Amendments to PD52C and PD54D to give effect to proposed changes in the judicial review process for NSIP cases.   Civil Procedure (Amendment No 2) Rules 2025 and supporting   Practice Direction Amendments   The Statutory Instrument  (SI) is published via the legislation website and the online rules will be updated in due course.    The Civil Procedure (Amendment No 2) Rules 2025 will enter into force, variously on 12 th September 2025 and on 1 st October 2025 as set out in the instrument.  The SI contains amendments in respect of the following:    Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 – A suite of amendments are being made to various rules and PDs in consequence of the Act.   E-working – A new PD, PD5C CE-File electronic filing and case management system, is being introduced to regularise and standardise the use of CE-File which has previously operated under a pilot PD (PD510).   Summary Assessment – Amendments are being made to the general rules about costs to provide flexibility to enable a judge, other than the hearing judge, to make a summary assessment of costs. This arises out of the developments in caselaw ( R(Isah) v Secretary of State [2023]).   Judicial Review of nationally significant infrastructure projects (NSIPs) – An amendment to CPR 52 to remove an unnecessary reference to section 18(1)(a) of the Senior Courts Act 1981 and to facilitate the implementation of Clause 12 in the Planning and Infrastructure Bill which, upon coming into force, will remove the right of appeal for cases deemed totally without merit at the oral permission hearing in the High Court.   Filing and Serving Written Evidence – A clarificatory amendment is being made to CPR 8.5 to provide for defendants who initially indicated an intention to contest jurisdiction, when submitting their acknowledgement of service (AoS), but then go on to defend the claim instead.  The rules now make it clear that the defendant has 14 days from the date of filing the AoS to file the defence evidence.  This arises in response to caselaw ( Fit Kitchens -v- Relx 2023 EWHC 1954 ).   Costs Orders (non-parties) – An addition to CPR 46.2 (new rule 46.2(3)) in response to an issue in practice. The amendment intends to clarify the procedure for when the court considers adding a party to proceedings for the purposes of costs only.   Appellants Notice – Amendment to CPR 52.12(3) to update the rule to reflect practice and increase the deadline for service of sealed appellant’s notice from seven to 14 days.    Closed Material Procedure (CMP) – Amendments to Part 82 to give effect to two recommendations from the 2022 Independent Report on the Operation of Closed Material Procedure under the Justice and Security Act 2013, undertaken by Sir Duncan Ouseley. The first, makes provision as to the circumstances in which the court may direct the relevant person to provide a draft closed defence or draft closed summary to assist with determining a closed material application. The second, codifies an existing informal practice which allows for communication requests to be agreed between parties, without the involvement of the court.   Tidying Up – Various other minor amendments, such as amendments to Part 62 regarding arbitration claims, by deleting the out-of-date reference following legislative changes and the introduction of the Arbitration Act 2025 . 188th PD Making Document (PDF) The 186th  Practice Direction Update The 186 th Practice Direction (PD) Update comes into force on 1 st October 2025. The Master of the Rolls and the Courts Minister have signed the Practice Direction Update, which contains a suite of amendments in support of the Civil Procedure (Amendment No 2) Rules 2025 (above) as well as providing for the following:    Uprating the Litigant in Person (LiP) hourly rate – PD46 (cost, special cases) the rule which prescribes the hourly rate is being uprated, along with a transitional provision in support.   Foreign Process Section (FPS) – Amendments to PD6B and PD34A to support a package of reforms to improve the service provided to users; primarily by making references to the FPS and Royal Courts Justice (RCJ) consistent within the rules.   Ad
